title: Exploring a positive future for AI music: 'David Bowie would not be fearful of this' - Music Ally
text:
This is news coverage of
Music Ally Connect 2026
– our international music business conference that is taking place 22–23 January 2026 in London.
It won’t surprise you to hear that AI was the first big topic at our Music Ally Connect conference today.
The keynote from UMG’s Jonathan Dworkin
was followed by a panel discussing how GenAI music might evolve – and also how it might be
truly
useful (and not harmful) for human musicians. Nadine Kroher, co-founder of Passion Labs, got things started by focusing on the rapid pace of improvement.
“It’s really interesting how fast this field has evolved. When we compare it to, for example, music understanding, we still can’t track beats in classical music, we still can’t transcribe music reliably, but we
can
generate music that fools 97% of people!”
She was referring to
Deezer’s recent study
that claimed exactly this: that when it played people a mixture of AI-generated and human tracks, 97% couldn’t tell the difference.
Kroher noted that current models come with by-design limitations: they can’t create music that is “super-novel or super-emotional” because they were never optimised to do that. She highlighted two areas of interesting development.
“What people are now working on is controllability. Is there a better way to drive a generative model than writing a prompt, then writing another prompt, and ending up with very long prompts? And that’s not only in music,” she said.
The second aspect is speed, since currently there is a delay between prompting a musical AI and receiving the output back.
“Is there a way of making these models fast enough so that they can produce in near real-time? That could be interesting: for people having some kind of a live concert that can be GenAI-supported.”
Wish you were here?
If you’re not able to attend
Music Ally Connect
, we will be releasing a high-quality video recording of the whole event. Please contact
anthony@musically.com
to find out more!
Mateusz Modrzejewski, assistant professor at Politechnika Warszawska, agreed that the current limitations are down to how AI-music models have been designed.
“They got the users they were built for. All the research efforts went into designing a tool that works in a way that you press a button and you get a song,” he said.
“You could use those same research capabilities and compute etc to design a plethora of other tools, like text-to-stems, or arrangement-to-variations. Text-to-finished-song is not the only thing neural networks can do.”
Simon Wheeler, director of global commercial strategy at Beggars Group,  said that his company is taking a hard look at the different AI-music tools and services in order to decide which ones will be genuinely additive for its business and its artists.
“If we’re thinking about AI music as ‘is it going to be good enough to press a button and output a track which is going to replace humans?’, well, what’s the point? What are we trying to achieve with that?” he said.
“If there’s one problem the world
doesn’t
have right now, it’s not having a shitload of music which no one listens to! There’s a quarter of a billion tracks on streaming services, the vast majority of which are either not listened to or hardly listened to.”
Wheeler talked about generative music for games as a much more interesting area – “that’s not replacing anything which is currently happening” – and suggested that it is up to the music industry in partnership with GenAI startups to find other positive use cases.
“We’ve got to be careful as an industry that we’re not sort of like: oh well, we’ve got to do this whatever and just license anything which comes along,” he said.
“If we approach this in a thoughtful manner, then we’re going to generate something which is additive, which is positive, which is creative. And then there’s some pretty exciting things in a future which isn’t so scary.”
“This is the earliest days. The tools aren’t out there to really delight people, to give them a great experience”
Simon Wheeler, beggars Group
Many artists have reservations about AI in various ways, but Wheeler warned against seeing artists en masse as resistant to new technologies.
“Artists have always embraced new tools and new technologies. Not everyone, but the ones who are curious. I always think that if David Bowie was with us now, he’d be absolutely all over this stuff. Without a shadow of a doubt. One of the world’s greatest artists who has ever, ever emerged. He would not be fearful of this.”
“Artists love to break stuff,” agreed Modrzejewski. “If you give them new expressive gear, they will find all the new creative ways to break stuff and make new sounds with it.”
The panel finished by talking about whether the wider backlash against AI (and some of the Big Tech moguls pushing it) will spill over into how musicians and the music industry use GenAI music technologies.
“This is the earliest days. The tools aren’t out there to really delight people, to give them a great experience which they’re going to enjoy at the moment. It’s largely ‘press a button or put a prompt in, get a song out’ and associated with AI slop, which is not helpful for anyone,” said Wheeler.
“So why do people hate it? Well, of course, that’s AI stop. But hopefully we and the people that the industry work with will come up with tools, with products, that consumers engage with and find real pleasure and delight in,” he continued.
“And then the sentiment changes, because they have had a really culturally rich and enjoyable experience. That’s what we’ve got to get to, but we’re not there yet: none of these things are out of the door.”
Kroher talked about one of the challenges facing the music industry – and particularly streaming services – which is deciding what to classify as ‘AI music’.
“There is ‘I just prompted one of those machines, used the output and put it on Spotify. And obviously I didn’t do that once: I wrote a script that does that 25,000 times a day’,” she said – just to be clear,
Kroher
hasn’t done this: she was just describing one use case that could easily be labelled as AI-generated music.
“Then on the other extreme, it’s ‘Okay, I wrote a song completely myself, and just at one point in the lyrics I got stuck, I asked ChatGPT, it proposed five or 10 examples for one line in the lyrics, and I decided to pick the second one but I changed the words’. Should that be labelled as AI-generated content?” she said.
“And then how do you measure this? I think the problem is not necessarily AI-generated content. It’s the spamming of streaming services, and also things that are deceptive.”
The panel’s moderator, Neutune chief industry and rights officer Virginie Berger, offered her own concerns, citing the ‘
dead internet theory
‘ – which suggests that the majority of content online is (or will soon be) generated by AI and bots rather than by humans.
“
The most-subscribed-to-streamer is AI
. The majority of articles on the internet are written by AI. One in three [newly-uploaded] songs on Deezer is AI. Four of the top 10 YouTube channels are AI…
over 20% of YouTube videos [shown to new users] are now AI slop
… so AI is eating everything human!” she said.
Are there any reasons for optimism? Wheeler said that it may come down to collective agreement and actions within the music industry.
“Everyone has got a role to play in formulating a world which we want to live in as a music industry. Do we want a polluted pool full of any old slop that people can put in there, and it’s ‘make sense of it yourself’? It doesn’t really sound that exciting,” he said.
“Or do we want a healthy music ecosystem where the people that create the value, the artists, can get rewarded for the work that they do as fairly and as completely as possible? That’s where we’re coming from. It’s probably not going to be this perfect world. I hope it’s not going to be this anything-goes polluted world.”
“The distributors that are putting a huge amount of this stuff onto services, their business model, a lot of them, is driven by the volume of what they put on there. They’ve got a massive role to play in this,” he added. “Do they just want to keep pumping things out and thinking it’s all the same and that’s good? We all need to get aligned on what we think, and work towards what that solution is.”
Modrzejewski offered a final word on the challenges for the industry and for regulators.
“We can see already that good models came way before good governance, and we’re just finding out what are the questions we should actually ask,” he said, before predicting that more research is needed into “detection and attribution and explainability and interpretability” in these models.
“It’s such a fascinating research area, but that will also improve the questions we will be able to ask, and how to move forward.”
Many thanks to our Connect 2026
event sponsors
AudioSalad, Innovate UK, Kobalt, and Musixmatch – and to all of our many other Partner, Track, Lanyard, Support, Ally and Reception Sponsors.
Stuart Dredge
Music Ally's Head of Insight
More by Stuart Dredge
